title: Chicken tagine with preserved lemon and olives
subtitle: Moroccan braised chicken with salty-sour preserved lemon
excerpt: Chicken pieces braised with preserved lemon, green olives, saffron, ginger, and coriander in a golden, fragrant sauce. The preserved lemon is the defining ingredient: salted, fermented, and intensely citrus-forward in a way that fresh lemon cannot replicate. One of the most widely known Moroccan dishes outside Morocco.
sourceNotes: Chicken with preserved lemon and olives is the dish most associated with Moroccan cooking in the European imagination. Paula Wolfert's Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco (1973) brought it to wide English-language attention; Claudia Roden's A Book of Middle Eastern Food followed. The combination of saffron, ginger, and preserved lemon defines the Fassi (Fez) cooking tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well. Add fresh coriander after reheating.

content.text: Preserved lemon must be used correctly. Rinse the lemon under cold water, then cut in half and scrape out the flesh — the pale inner pith and flesh are discarded. Only the rind goes into the dish. Slice the rind finely or roughly chop it. The salt-fermented rind has a completely different flavour from fresh lemon zest: softer, less sharp, with an almost floral intensity.

Bone-in chicken pieces give more flavour than boneless. Thighs and legs work best; breast dries out more easily over the long braise. If using a whole jointed chicken, add the breast pieces 20 minutes after the legs and thighs.

Combine the {{chicken-thigh}} with the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{turmeric}}, {{black-pepper}}, and half the {{coriander}}. Cover and refrigerate for at least 1 hour. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide, heavy casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the chicken in batches, skin-side down for 4 to 5 minutes, until golden. Remove and set aside. Add the {{onion}} to the pot and cook for 8 minutes. Return the chicken, add the saffron water and 200 ml of water. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 35 minutes. Add the {{preserved-lemon}} rind and {{olives-green}}. Cook uncovered for a further 10 minutes to reduce the sauce. Scatter over the remaining {{coriander}} and serve.

items.cause: Preserved lemon not rinsed, or using more than the rind
items.fix: Rinse the preserved lemon under cold water before use and discard the flesh entirely. The rind should season the dish, not overpower it

items.cause: Too much water added or lid left on throughout
items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 10 to 15 minutes and reduce over medium heat

items.symptom: Chicken skin soft rather than golden
items.fix: Dry the chicken skin thoroughly before browning. Start skin-side down in a hot pan — the skin must be golden before flipping

content.text: Chicken tagine with preserved lemon and olives has become the ambassador dish of Moroccan cooking internationally. In Morocco it is a Friday dish (made for the family lunch after prayer) and is found in homes and restaurants across the country. In Britain it appears on restaurant menus and in home cooking alike, partly because preserved lemons are now widely sold in supermarkets and partly because the dish is not difficult to make well. The preserved lemon is available online and in most supermarkets, though Moroccan and Middle Eastern shops stock better-quality versions.
